fr:title.text: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition ›

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition ›

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By Lemma , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in Example :
It is not difficult to show using Definition that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.

fr:title.text: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By Lemma , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in Example :
It is not difficult to show using Definition that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.

fr:title.text: Coherent remainder as a path object

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Coherent remainder as a path object

fr:li.fr:p: We exhibit the latter as a right-cloven family of path objects over the former with the cleaving that for \eta _0,\eta _1\colon g\circ f\sim 1_{A} and \eta _{01}\colon \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 defines \eta _{01}^*\colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _1\sim \epsilon \circ f \to \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _0\sim \epsilon \circ f as follows:

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: With this cleaving in mind, finally define \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma to be the following contravariant sum of path objects:
Any biinvertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.
As biinvertible maps are quasi-invertible and quasi-invertible maps are contractible.
If a type A is n-truncated, then it is also n+1-truncated.
By induction on the truncation level n, with two base cases given by Lemma and Lemma .
If B is a retract of A and A is a proposition, then B is a proposition.
This can be seen using the cancellation laws of retractions.
When s\colon A\to B is a section of r\colon B\to A, we say that A is a retract of B.

A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x\colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.
The fiber of a function f\colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a\colon A equipped with identifications p\colon fa=b.

A function f\colon A \to B is called n-truncated when all of its fibers are n-truncated types. By analogy, we shall also refer to maps as contractible when their fibers are; we shall refer to maps as propositional when their fibers are propositions.

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b\colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \phi ;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
A biinverse to a function f\colon A \to B is defined to be a pair of a section and a retraction to f. We will refer to a function f\colon A\to B equipped with a biinverse as a biinvertible function.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.
